title: Raspberry Fool
subtitle: Raspberries pressed through a sieve and folded through whipped cream
excerpt: Raspberries sweetened with sugar and pushed through a sieve to remove the seeds, giving a smooth, intensely coloured coulis that is folded through softly whipped double cream. Served cold in glasses. The seedless coulis gives a smoother result than a strawberry fool.
sourceNotes: The raspberry fool follows the same structural tradition as all British fools, fruit and cream, but the smaller seeds of the raspberry make sieving worthwhile, giving a smooth, deeply coloured cream that is distinct from the cruder-textured strawberry version.

content.text: The difference between a raspberry fool and a strawberry fool is the sieving step. Raspberry seeds are large enough to be unpleasant in a cream dessert, so the crushed fruit is pressed through a fine sieve to leave a smooth, deeply red coulis. This takes five minutes and makes the finished texture considerably better.

Reserve about a quarter of the raspberries whole before crushing, they get folded through at the end for texture. The coulis can be made a day ahead and kept in the fridge; the cream is whipped and folded just before serving or a few hours ahead.

Reserve about 80 g of the raspberries to one side. Crush the remaining {{raspberries}} of raspberries with {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar and the lemon juice in a bowl. Press through a fine sieve, pushing with the back of a spoon, until only dry seeds remain. Discard the seeds. The coulis should be smooth and very dark red.

Whip {{double-cream}} of double cream to soft peaks. Pour in the raspberry coulis and fold gently until combined, the mixture should be pale pink-red with visible streaks. Fold through the reserved whole raspberries.
Spoon into glasses and chill for at least 1 hour. Serve with shortbread or tuile biscuits.

items.fix: Whip cream to soft peaks only. Allow the coulis to come to room temperature before folding into the cream.
items.cause: Cream was over-whipped, or the coulis was added when still very cold
items.symptom: Fool is grainy or the cream separated

items.fix: Underripe raspberries yield less juice. Add 1 tablespoon of water to the crushed raspberries and leave to macerate for 15 minutes before sieving.
items.cause: The raspberries were dry or underripe
items.symptom: Very little coulis made from the raspberries

items.fix: Add an extra tablespoon of caster sugar to the coulis. Taste before folding in the cream.
items.cause: Raspberries were very tart, or too much lemon juice was used

items.fix: Fold the coulis with minimal strokes so that streaks of white cream remain visible against the red. The fool should look marbled, not uniform.
items.cause: Coulis was fully incorporated rather than loosely folded
items.symptom: Texture is too uniform and mousse-like

content.text: Raspberry fool sits within the same British tradition as gooseberry and strawberry fool. Fruit and cream in the simplest possible form. The raspberry version benefits from the sieving step, which the strawberry version can skip. The result is a cleaner texture and a more intense colour. Raspberry and cream has been a standard pairing in British summer cooking for centuries, in many forms: syllabubs, creams, and the more modern fool.
